quiz_questions.question_text: What are the main phases of the cell cycle?

options.text: Meiosis I and meiosis II
G₁, S, G₂ (interphase) and M phase (mitosis + cytokinesis)
Prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase only
S phase and M phase only

quiz_questions.explanation: The cell cycle has two major parts: interphase and the mitotic (M) phase. Interphase includes G₁ (cell growth and normal function), S phase (DNA synthesis/replication), and G₂ (further growth and preparation for division). The M phase includes mitosis (nuclear division through PMAT stages) and cytokinesis (cytoplasm division). Most cells spend about 90% of their cycle in interphase.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens during each sub-phase of interphase (G₁, S, G₂)?

options.text: All three phases involve chromosome separation
G₁: DNA replicates; S: cell grows; G₂: chromosomes condense
G₁: cell grows and performs normal functions; S: DNA is replicated; G₂: cell continues growing and prepares organelles and proteins for mitosis
G₁ and G₂ are identical phases where cells rest

quiz_questions.explanation: Interphase is the longest part of the cell cycle (~90%). G₁ (Gap 1): the cell grows, produces proteins, and carries out normal metabolic activities. S (Synthesis): DNA is replicated so each chromosome becomes two sister chromatids joined at the centromere. G₂ (Gap 2): the cell continues growing, synthesizes proteins needed for mitosis (like tubulin for spindle fibers), and checks for DNA replication errors before committing to division.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are cell cycle checkpoints, and what are the three main ones?

options.text: Points where cells automatically speed up division to meet the demands of growing tissues and organs
The three phases of mitosis: prophase, metaphase, and anaphase, which are the visible stages of division
Control points that either allow or halt cell cycle progression: G₁ checkpoint (cell size/DNA damage), G₂ checkpoint (DNA replication complete?), and M checkpoint (spindle attachment correct?)
Locations in the cell where chromosomes are stored between divisions inside the nuclear membrane

quiz_questions.explanation: Cell cycle checkpoints act as quality-control gates that check whether critical events have been completed correctly before allowing progression to the next phase. The G₁ checkpoint (restriction point) checks for cell size, available nutrients, growth factor signals, and DNA integrity. The G₂ checkpoint verifies that DNA replication is complete and error-free. The M checkpoint (spindle assembly checkpoint/SAC) ensures all chromosomes are properly attached to spindle fibers before anaphase begins.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the role of p53 in the cell cycle?

options.text: p53 forms the mitotic spindle during cell division by organizing tubulin into microtubule structures
p53 is a tumor suppressor protein that detects DNA damage and either halts the cell cycle for repair or triggers apoptosis (programmed cell death) if damage is irreparable
p53 replicates DNA during S phase by acting as a helicase that unwinds the double helix at replication forks
p53 promotes rapid cell division by activating oncogenes that drive progression through the cell cycle

quiz_questions.explanation: p53 is called the 'guardian of the genome' because it responds to DNA damage by activating repair pathways or triggering apoptosis. When DNA damage is detected, p53 activates p21, which inhibits CDK-cyclin complexes and halts the cell cycle at the G₁ checkpoint. If the damage cannot be repaired, p53 activates pro-apoptotic genes (like BAX), causing the cell to self-destruct. About 50% of all human cancers have mutated p53.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between a proto-oncogene and an oncogene?

options.text: Oncogenes are normal genes that only promote apoptosis
Proto-oncogenes cause cancer; oncogenes prevent it
Both terms refer to the same type of tumor suppressor gene
A proto-oncogene is a normal gene that promotes cell growth and division; when mutated, it becomes an oncogene that drives uncontrolled cell proliferation

quiz_questions.explanation: Proto-oncogenes are normal, essential genes that encode proteins promoting cell growth, division, and survival (like growth factor receptors, signal transduction proteins, and transcription factors). They are the 'accelerator' of the cell cycle. When a proto-oncogene is mutated (by point mutation, gene amplification, or chromosomal translocation), it becomes an oncogene — a permanently activated version that drives continuous cell proliferation regardless of normal signals. Examples include RAS (signal transduction) and HER2 (growth factor receptor).

quiz_questions.question_text: What do tumor suppressor genes do, and what happens when they are mutated?

options.text: They promote cell division; when mutated they slow growth and prevent cells from dividing at normal rates
They suppress the immune system's response to tumors, preventing the body from attacking cancerous cells
They only function during embryonic development and are permanently silenced in all adult somatic cells
They normally slow cell division, repair DNA, or trigger apoptosis; when both copies are mutated/lost, these 'brakes' fail and cells can proliferate uncontrollably

quiz_questions.explanation: Tumor suppressor genes act as the 'brakes' of the cell cycle. Their protein products inhibit cell division (like Rb blocking the G₁/S transition), repair DNA damage, or trigger apoptosis when damage is severe (like p53). Unlike oncogenes, BOTH copies must be inactivated for function to be lost (Knudson's two-hit hypothesis). Examples: p53 (guardian of the genome), Rb (retinoblastoma protein), BRCA1/BRCA2 (DNA repair), APC (colon cancer suppressor).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is apoptosis, and why is it important for preventing cancer?

options.text: Apoptosis is the process of DNA replication before mitosis
Apoptosis is programmed cell death — a controlled, orderly process that eliminates damaged, infected, or unnecessary cells before they can become cancerous
Apoptosis is accidental cell death from physical injury (necrosis)
Apoptosis is cell division that produces cancer

quiz_questions.explanation: Apoptosis is programmed cell death — a highly regulated process where the cell shrinks, its DNA is fragmented by enzymes (caspases), and it is neatly packaged into 'apoptotic bodies' that are absorbed by neighboring cells or macrophages without causing inflammation. It is essential for preventing cancer because cells with irreparable DNA damage can self-destruct before they accumulate the mutations needed for tumor formation. Cancer cells often evade apoptosis through mutations in p53 or overexpression of anti-apoptotic proteins like Bcl-2.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between a benign tumor and a malignant tumor?

options.text: Both types are identical and equally dangerous
Malignant tumors stay in one place; benign tumors spread
Benign tumors remain localized and do not invade other tissues; malignant tumors invade surrounding tissue and can metastasize (spread to distant body parts)
Benign tumors are always dangerous; malignant tumors are harmless

quiz_questions.explanation: Benign tumors are abnormal growths that remain encapsulated, grow slowly, and do not invade surrounding tissue or spread to distant sites. They are generally not life-threatening (though they can cause problems by pressing on nearby structures). Malignant tumors (cancers) are invasive — they break through tissue boundaries, stimulate blood vessel growth (angiogenesis), and can metastasize by releasing cells into the blood or lymphatic system that establish secondary tumors in distant organs.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is metastasis?

options.text: The shrinkage of a tumor after chemotherapy
The programmed death of cancer cells during treatment
The spread of cancer cells from the primary tumor to distant parts of the body through the blood or lymphatic system, forming secondary tumors
The initial formation of a tumor from a single mutant cell

quiz_questions.explanation: Metastasis is the process by which cancer cells break away from the primary tumor, enter the bloodstream or lymphatic system, travel to distant organs, and establish new (secondary) tumors. This is what makes cancer most dangerous and difficult to treat. Metastatic cells must acquire several capabilities: detachment from the primary tumor, invasion through the basement membrane, survival in circulation, and colonization of new tissue. Common metastatic sites include lungs, liver, brain, and bones.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are carcinogens, and how do they contribute to cancer development?

options.text: Carcinogens are medications used to treat cancer by targeting rapidly dividing cells with cytotoxic drugs
Carcinogens are beneficial substances that strengthen DNA repair mechanisms and prevent mutations from occurring
Carcinogens are agents that cause cancer by damaging DNA or disrupting cell division — examples include UV radiation, tobacco smoke chemicals, and certain viruses (like HPV)
Carcinogens are proteins that prevent tumor growth by activating the p53 checkpoint and triggering apoptosis

quiz_questions.explanation: Carcinogens are agents that increase cancer risk by causing DNA mutations or promoting uncontrolled cell division. They include chemical carcinogens (benzene, formaldehyde, tobacco tar compounds like benzo[a]pyrene), physical carcinogens (UV radiation causing thymine dimers, ionizing radiation breaking DNA strands), and biological carcinogens (HPV causing cervical cancer, Hepatitis B/C causing liver cancer, H. pylori causing stomach cancer). Carcinogens typically act over years, accumulating mutations through repeated exposure.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a tumor suppressor gene?

options.text: A gene found only in cancer cells
A gene that promotes rapid cell growth and division when activated
A gene whose protein product slows cell division, repairs DNA damage, or triggers apoptosis — acting as a 'brake' on the cell cycle (e.g., p53, Rb, BRCA1)
A gene that suppresses the immune system's ability to detect tumors

quiz_questions.explanation: Tumor suppressor genes encode proteins that regulate cell growth by slowing division, repairing DNA, or inducing apoptosis. They function as protective 'brakes' on the cell cycle. Key examples: p53 detects DNA damage and triggers repair or death; Rb blocks the G₁/S transition until growth signals are confirmed; BRCA1/2 repair double-strand DNA breaks; APC regulates the Wnt signaling pathway. Cancer typically requires loss of BOTH copies of a tumor suppressor (Knudson's two-hit hypothesis), unlike oncogenes which need only one activating mutation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes an oncogene?

options.text: A gene that suppresses tumor formation by triggering apoptosis
A gene that protects cells from carcinogens
A mutated or overexpressed form of a proto-oncogene that promotes uncontrolled cell growth and division, even without normal growth signals
A gene that only activates during embryonic development

quiz_questions.explanation: An oncogene is a gain-of-function mutation of a normal proto-oncogene. While proto-oncogenes help regulate normal cell growth, oncogenes are permanently 'switched on' — they continuously signal the cell to divide regardless of external growth factors or checkpoint controls. Oncogenes act like a stuck accelerator pedal. Common oncogenes include mutant RAS (found in ~30% of cancers), amplified HER2 (breast cancer), and the BCR-ABL fusion gene (chronic myelogenous leukemia). Only ONE mutated copy is sufficient to drive cancer (dominant mutation).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes apoptosis?

options.text: Uncontrolled, accidental cell death caused by physical injury or infection (necrosis)
The process of cell division during the M phase
Programmed cell death — a controlled, orderly self-destruction process that safely eliminates damaged, infected, or unnecessary cells without causing inflammation
The growth of new blood vessels toward a tumor (angiogenesis)

quiz_questions.explanation: Apoptosis (programmed cell death) is a tightly regulated 'cellular suicide' mechanism. Triggered by internal signals (DNA damage, p53 activation) or external signals (death receptor ligands like FasL or TNF), it activates a cascade of caspase enzymes that systematically disassemble the cell. The cell shrinks, its DNA is fragmented, and it is packaged into membrane-bound apoptotic bodies that phagocytes cleanly engulf. Unlike necrosis (messy, inflammatory cell death), apoptosis is clean and inflammation-free. About 50-70 billion cells undergo apoptosis daily in an adult human.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a benign tumor?

options.text: A tumor that has been successfully treated with chemotherapy and is no longer actively growing in the patient
A cancerous mass that invades surrounding tissue and metastasizes to distant organs through the bloodstream
An abnormal cell mass that remains localized, is usually encapsulated, does not invade surrounding tissue or spread to other body parts, and is generally not life-threatening
Any type of tumor, whether cancerous or not, including both localized growths and metastatic cancer masses

quiz_questions.explanation: A benign tumor is a non-cancerous growth that remains enclosed in a fibrous capsule and does not invade neighboring tissue or metastasize. Benign tumors typically grow slowly, have well-defined borders, and retain some resemblance to normal tissue. While generally not life-threatening, benign tumors can still cause problems depending on their location — for example, a benign brain tumor can create dangerous pressure on brain structures, and benign thyroid nodules can affect hormone production. Common examples include lipomas (fat tissue), fibroids (uterus), and moles.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a malignant tumor?

options.text: A non-cancerous growth that stays in one location and is fully encapsulated by connective tissue barriers
A cancerous mass of cells that can invade surrounding tissues, break through basement membranes, stimulate angiogenesis, and metastasize to distant organs via the bloodstream or lymphatic system
A cluster of dead cells that the body will naturally absorb through immune-mediated phagocytic clearance
A temporary swelling caused by infection that resolves once the immune response eliminates the pathogen

quiz_questions.explanation: A malignant tumor (cancer) is characterized by several dangerous properties: (1) uncontrolled proliferation ignoring normal growth signals, (2) invasion of surrounding tissues by breaking through the extracellular matrix and basement membrane, (3) angiogenesis — stimulating new blood vessel growth to supply the tumor, (4) metastasis — spreading through blood or lymph to colonize distant organs. Malignant cells are often poorly differentiated (look abnormal), have irregular nuclei, and show high mitotic index. Treatment typically requires surgery, radiation, chemotherapy, or targeted therapy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a carcinogen?

options.text: A medication that kills cancer cells by disrupting their ability to divide and replicate their DNA
Any agent (chemical, radiation, or biological) that can cause cancer by damaging DNA, inducing mutations, or disrupting normal cell cycle regulation
A type of benign tumor that never becomes cancerous and poses no risk to the patient's overall health
A protein produced by tumors to suppress the immune system and evade detection by cytotoxic T cells

quiz_questions.explanation: A carcinogen is any substance or agent that promotes cancer formation. Chemical carcinogens include tobacco tar compounds (benzo[a]pyrene), asbestos fibers, and benzene. Physical carcinogens include UV radiation (causes thymine dimers) and ionizing radiation (breaks DNA strands). Biological carcinogens include viruses that insert their DNA near proto-oncogenes or disable tumor suppressors — HPV (cervical cancer), Hepatitis B/C (liver cancer), EBV (Burkitt's lymphoma). Carcinogens typically require repeated exposure over time to accumulate enough mutations for cancer.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes metastasis?

options.text: The encapsulation of a benign tumor by connective tissue, preventing it from invading surrounding structures
The death of cancer cells through chemotherapy drugs that target rapidly dividing cell populations specifically
The initial formation of a tumor in one location from a single cell that has accumulated oncogenic mutations
The process by which cancer cells break away from the primary tumor, travel through the blood or lymphatic system, and establish secondary tumors in distant organs

quiz_questions.explanation: Metastasis is a multi-step process: (1) cancer cells lose cell-cell adhesion (downregulate E-cadherin), (2) they secrete enzymes (matrix metalloproteinases) to degrade the basement membrane and extracellular matrix, (3) they intravasate into blood vessels or lymphatics, (4) they survive circulation and evade immune cells, (5) they arrest at distant capillary beds, (6) they extravasate and colonize new tissue. Metastasis is the primary cause of cancer mortality, responsible for ~90% of cancer deaths.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a proto-oncogene?

options.text: A gene that suppresses tumor formation by repairing DNA damage and inducing apoptosis in abnormal cells
A viral gene inserted into human DNA by retroviruses that immediately begins producing oncogenic proteins
A normal gene that encodes proteins promoting cell growth and division — it becomes a cancer-causing oncogene when mutated, amplified, or translocated
A gene that always causes cancer when present in any cell, regardless of whether it is mutated or not

quiz_questions.explanation: Proto-oncogenes are essential, normal genes present in all cells. They encode growth factors, growth factor receptors, signal transduction proteins, and transcription factors that regulate cell growth and division. They become oncogenes through activating mutations: point mutations (e.g., RAS), gene amplification (extra copies, e.g., HER2 in breast cancer), or chromosomal translocation (e.g., Philadelphia chromosome creating BCR-ABL in CML). The key distinction is that proto-oncogenes are normal and necessary; oncogenes are their mutant, overactive forms.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a cyclin-dependent kinase (CDK)?

options.text: A receptor on the cell surface that detects growth factors and transmits signals to the nucleus for division
A protein that directly replicates DNA during S phase by catalyzing the addition of complementary nucleotides
A structural protein that forms the mitotic spindle by polymerizing into microtubules during cell division
An enzyme that, when activated by binding a cyclin protein, phosphorylates target proteins to drive the cell cycle forward through specific transitions (G₁→S, G₂→M)

quiz_questions.explanation: CDKs are a family of protein kinases that serve as the core molecular 'engines' driving cell cycle progression. CDKs are present throughout the cell cycle but are only active when bound to their specific cyclin partner (which rises and falls in level at specific phases). For example, CDK4/6-Cyclin D drives G₁ progression, CDK2-Cyclin E triggers the G₁/S transition, and CDK1-Cyclin B (MPF) triggers mitotic entry. CDK inhibitors (like p21, activated by p53) can halt the cycle at checkpoints.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes angiogenesis in the context of cancer?

options.text: The spreading of cancer through the lymphatic system only, without involvement of the circulatory blood system
The formation of a protective capsule around a benign tumor that prevents it from invading nearby tissues
The programmed death of cancer cells through the activation of caspase enzymes and DNA fragmentation signals
The formation of new blood vessels that tumors stimulate to supply themselves with oxygen and nutrients, enabling growth beyond 1-2 mm and providing routes for metastasis

quiz_questions.explanation: Angiogenesis is the growth of new blood vessels from existing ones. Without a blood supply, tumors cannot grow beyond about 1-2 mm in diameter because cells too far from capillaries lack oxygen and nutrients. Cancer cells stimulate angiogenesis by secreting vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and other pro-angiogenic signals. The new blood vessels are abnormal (leaky, disorganized) but provide the tumor with resources and pathways for metastasis. Anti-angiogenic drugs (like bevacizumab/Avastin) target VEGF to starve tumors of their blood supply.

quiz_questions.question_text: A family has a history where multiple members develop different types of cancer (breast, brain, leukemia, bone) at unusually young ages. Genetic testing reveals that affected members carry a germline mutation in one copy of the TP53 gene (Li-Fraumeni syndrome). Why does losing one copy of p53 dramatically increase cancer risk across many tissue types?

options.text: With one inherited defective copy, only ONE additional somatic mutation in the remaining p53 allele is needed to completely lose p53 function — since p53 protects virtually all cell types by detecting DNA damage and triggering repair or apoptosis, losing it leaves every tissue vulnerable to uncontrolled growth
The mutation causes all cells to immediately become cancerous as soon as the inherited defective copy is present
One copy of p53 is sufficient to cause cancer directly without requiring any additional somatic mutations
p53 mutations only affect bone tissue because p53 is exclusively expressed in osteoblast cells of the skeleton

quiz_questions.explanation: Li-Fraumeni syndrome illustrates Knudson's two-hit hypothesis for tumor suppressors. Normally, both copies of p53 must be inactivated for function to be lost. Patients with LFS inherit one defective copy in every cell, so only one somatic mutation (the 'second hit') in any cell is needed to eliminate p53 entirely. Since p53 is the 'guardian of the genome' active in all cell types, its loss removes the key checkpoint that detects DNA damage and triggers apoptosis. This explains why LFS patients develop diverse cancer types at young ages — any tissue can be affected.

quiz_questions.question_text: A breast cancer patient's tumor biopsy shows overexpression of the HER2 protein (a growth factor receptor). Her doctor prescribes trastuzumab (Herceptin), a monoclonal antibody that specifically targets HER2. How does this targeted therapy differ from traditional chemotherapy in its mechanism?

options.text: Trastuzumab repairs the mutated HER2 gene so cells return to normal growth patterns and stop dividing

Trastuzumab specifically binds to HER2 receptors on cancer cells, blocking the growth signals that drive proliferation — unlike chemotherapy which targets all rapidly dividing cells (including healthy ones), targeted therapy selectively attacks cancer cells expressing the specific molecular target
Trastuzumab strengthens the immune system nonspecifically, boosting overall cancer surveillance mechanisms
Trastuzumab kills all rapidly dividing cells like chemotherapy, including both cancerous and healthy cells

quiz_questions.explanation: HER2 is an oncogene — its amplification produces excess growth factor receptors that continuously signal cell proliferation. Trastuzumab (Herceptin) is a targeted therapy: a monoclonal antibody that binds specifically to the extracellular domain of HER2, blocking receptor dimerization and signaling. It also marks cancer cells for immune destruction (ADCC). Unlike chemotherapy (which poisons all rapidly dividing cells, causing side effects like hair loss, nausea, and immune suppression), targeted therapies attack specific molecular drivers of cancer with fewer off-target effects.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pathologist examines two tissue biopsy slides. Slide A shows a well-defined mass with cells that closely resemble normal tissue, surrounded by a fibrous capsule, with rare mitotic figures. Slide B shows irregular borders, cells with large abnormal nuclei, many mitotic figures, and cells invading through the basement membrane. How should the pathologist classify each sample?

options.text: Slide A: malignant tumor; Slide B: benign tumor -- the organized appearance indicates active invasion
Both are malignant tumors with different growth rates determined by the number of oncogene mutations
Neither slide shows a tumor because both tissues are normal with standard cell morphology and organization
Slide A: benign tumor (encapsulated, well-differentiated, non-invasive, low mitotic index); Slide B: malignant tumor (poorly differentiated, invasive through basement membrane, high mitotic index)

quiz_questions.explanation: Pathologists distinguish benign from malignant tumors based on several criteria: (1) Differentiation — benign cells resemble normal tissue (well-differentiated), while malignant cells look abnormal (poorly differentiated/anaplastic). (2) Growth pattern — benign tumors are encapsulated with smooth borders; malignant tumors have irregular, invasive borders. (3) Mitotic index — benign tumors have few dividing cells; malignant tumors show many abnormal mitoses. (4) Invasion — the hallmark of malignancy is invasion through the basement membrane into surrounding tissue.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 30-year-long smoker develops lung cancer. His doctor explains that the cancer did not appear overnight — it resulted from decades of carcinogen exposure. Using the multi-hit model of carcinogenesis, how did accumulated mutations from tobacco smoke lead to cancer?

options.text: Tobacco smoke only causes lung infections, not genetic mutations, and the cancer developed independently
A single mutation from one cigarette immediately caused cancer without requiring any additional mutations
The cancer was inherited and unrelated to smoking, since lung cancer runs in the patient's family history

Over 30 years, tobacco carcinogens (like benzo[a]pyrene) repeatedly damaged DNA — mutations gradually accumulated in proto-oncogenes (activating them) and tumor suppressor genes (inactivating both copies), until the cell acquired enough mutations (typically 3-7) to bypass all growth controls and become cancerous

quiz_questions.explanation: The multi-hit (or multi-step) model of carcinogenesis explains that cancer requires the accumulation of multiple mutations over time (typically 3-7 driver mutations). Tobacco smoke contains over 70 known carcinogens that damage DNA with each exposure. Over decades, mutations accumulate: perhaps first a RAS oncogene activation, then p53 loss, then Rb inactivation, then activation of telomerase, etc. Each mutation confers a growth advantage, and the cell lineage progressively loses normal growth controls. This explains why cancer risk increases with both the duration and intensity of carcinogen exposure.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 25-year-old woman receives a Pap smear that detects abnormal cervical cells. Further testing reveals infection with human papillomavirus (HPV) types 16 and 18. Her doctor explains that HPV is a biological carcinogen. How do these 'high-risk' HPV strains promote cervical cancer at the molecular level?

options.text: HPV produces viral proteins E6 and E7 that inactivate the tumor suppressors p53 (E6 targets p53 for degradation) and Rb (E7 binds and inhibits Rb) — simultaneously removing both 'brakes' on the cell cycle, allowing uncontrolled proliferation
HPV prevents the immune system from reaching cervical cells by forming a physical barrier around the tissue
HPV directly destroys cervical tissue through inflammation and repeated cycles of cell death and regeneration
HPV inserts an oncogene that makes cells grow faster by activating the cell cycle at the G1 checkpoint

quiz_questions.explanation: High-risk HPV strains (types 16 and 18 cause ~70% of cervical cancers) produce two key oncoproteins: E6 binds p53 and targets it for ubiquitin-mediated degradation, eliminating the cell's ability to detect DNA damage or trigger apoptosis. E7 binds and inactivates the Rb protein, releasing the E2F transcription factor to drive cells past the G₁/S restriction point into uncontrolled DNA replication. Together, E6 and E7 simultaneously disable two critical tumor suppressors. The HPV vaccine prevents infection by these high-risk strains, preventing cervical cancer.

quiz_questions.question_text: A child is diagnosed with retinoblastoma (eye cancer). Genetic testing reveals the child inherited a mutant copy of the RB1 gene from a parent. Only one additional somatic mutation in the remaining normal RB1 copy was needed for cancer to develop. This pattern is described by what hypothesis, and how does it explain inherited vs sporadic cancer?

options.text: Retinoblastoma is caused by environmental carcinogens, not genetics, since children are exposed to UV light

Knudson's two-hit hypothesis — inherited retinoblastoma requires only ONE somatic mutation (second hit) because the first hit is already present in every cell from birth; sporadic retinoblastoma requires TWO somatic mutations in the same cell, which is rarer and typically occurs later
The multi-hit hypothesis requires seven or more mutations in multiple tumor suppressor genes to develop cancer
The oncogene hypothesis states that one activating mutation in RB1 directly causes cancer without further changes

quiz_questions.explanation: Knudson's two-hit hypothesis (1971) was developed specifically from studying retinoblastoma. For tumor suppressors like RB1, BOTH alleles must be inactivated for function to be lost. Inherited (familial) retinoblastoma: one defective RB1 allele is present in every cell from birth (first hit inherited); only one somatic mutation is needed (second hit), so cancer develops early, often in both eyes. Sporadic retinoblastoma: both hits must occur somatically in the same retinal cell, which is extremely rare — cancer develops later, usually in only one eye.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with chronic myelogenous leukemia (CML) has the Philadelphia chromosome — a translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22 that creates the BCR-ABL fusion oncogene. The oncologist prescribes imatinib (Gleevec), which specifically inhibits the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase. Within months, the patient's white blood cell count returns to normal. What makes this a landmark example of targeted cancer therapy?

options.text: Imatinib repairs the Philadelphia chromosome translocation by cutting and rejoining the misplaced DNA segments
Imatinib works by stimulating the patient's immune system to recognize and destroy CML cells more effectively

Imatinib was designed to specifically fit into the ATP-binding pocket of the BCR-ABL fusion kinase, blocking the exact molecular driver of CML — this precision targeting of a single oncogenic protein made it the first successful rationally designed cancer drug
Imatinib destroys all rapidly dividing cells like conventional chemotherapy, causing widespread tissue damage

quiz_questions.explanation: Imatinib (Gleevec, approved 2001) is a paradigm-shifting drug in cancer treatment. The Philadelphia chromosome's BCR-ABL fusion produces a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that continuously signals cell proliferation. Imatinib was rationally designed to fit into the ATP-binding site of BCR-ABL, blocking its kinase activity and shutting down the proliferative signal. CML patients on imatinib achieve ~95% remission rates. This success demonstrated that understanding the specific molecular driver of a cancer can lead to highly effective, targeted therapies with fewer side effects than broad chemotherapy.

quiz_questions.question_text: A cancer patient undergoing chemotherapy experiences severe side effects: hair loss, nausea, low blood cell counts, and mouth sores. Her doctor explains that chemotherapy drugs target rapidly dividing cells. Why do these specific side effects occur, and what do the affected tissues have in common?

options.text: Hair follicles, gut epithelium, bone marrow, and oral mucosa are among the most rapidly dividing normal tissues — chemotherapy cannot distinguish cancer cells from other fast-dividing cells, so these healthy tissues are collateral damage
Chemotherapy drugs are toxic to all cells equally regardless of division rate, affecting every tissue uniformly
Only cancer cells are affected, but the immune system overreacts to dying tumor cells and damages healthy tissue
The side effects are caused by the cancer itself, not the treatment, as tumor growth damages surrounding organs

quiz_questions.explanation: Traditional chemotherapy drugs (like cisplatin, doxorubicin, and taxanes) target cells that divide rapidly — which includes cancer cells but also normal cells with high turnover rates. Hair follicle cells (divide every 23-72 hours → hair loss/alopecia), intestinal epithelium (replaced every 3-5 days → nausea/diarrhea), bone marrow stem cells (continuous blood cell production → anemia, low WBCs, low platelets), and oral mucosa (fast turnover → mouth sores). These side effects are the fundamental limitation of non-targeted chemotherapy and the reason targeted therapies were developed.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher discovers that a cancer cell line has a homozygous deletion of the CDKN2A gene, which encodes the p16 protein (a CDK inhibitor). She teststo see whether adding a CDK4/6 inhibitor drug (like palbociclib) can compensate for the lost p16 function. What checkpoint is she trying to restore, and why?

options.text: The M checkpoint (spindle assembly checkpoint) because p16 controls spindle fiber attachment to kinetochores
The S phase checkpoint because p16 controls DNA replication by regulating the firing of replication origins

The G₁/S restriction point — p16 normally inhibits CDK4/6, preventing Rb phosphorylation and blocking the G₁→S transition; without p16, CDK4/6 is constitutively active and cells uncontrollably enter S phase; the CDK4/6 inhibitor drug replaces the lost p16 braking function
The G2/M checkpoint because p16 prevents entry into mitosis by inhibiting the cyclin B-CDK1 complex directly

quiz_questions.explanation: The p16/INK4a protein (encoded by CDKN2A) is a tumor suppressor that inhibits CDK4 and CDK6. Normally, p16 prevents CDK4/6 from phosphorylating Rb protein. When Rb is unphosphorylated, it binds and inhibits the E2F transcription factor, blocking S phase entry. When p16 is lost (one of the most commonly deleted tumor suppressors in cancer), CDK4/6 is hyperactive, Rb is continuously phosphorylated and inactivated, and cells bypass the G₁ checkpoint. CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) are FDA-approved for breast cancer and pharmacologically restore the G₁ brake that p16 loss removed.

quiz_questions.question_text: A woman who tests positive for a BRCA1 mutation (which impairs double-strand DNA break repair) considers prophylactic bilateral mastectomy to reduce her breast cancer risk. Her genetic counselor explains that carrying one mutant BRCA1 copy gives her a 60-80% lifetime risk of breast cancer, versus ~12% in the general population. Why does losing DNA repair capability so dramatically increase cancer risk?

options.text: BRCA1 normally repairs double-strand DNA breaks through homologous recombination — with impaired repair, DNA damage from normal metabolism accumulates uncorrected, greatly accelerating the rate at which cells acquire the additional mutations (in oncogenes and other tumor suppressors) needed for cancer development
The BRCA1 mutation causes the immune system to attack breast tissue through an autoimmune response mechanism
BRCA1 mutations only affect breast tissue, so other organs are not at risk of developing any type of cancer
BRCA1 mutations directly cause cells to become cancerous immediately upon inheriting the defective gene copy

quiz_questions.explanation: BRCA1 is a tumor suppressor involved in the homologous recombination repair (HRR) pathway for double-strand DNA breaks — the most dangerous type of DNA damage. When BRCA1 function is lost (second hit in a BRCA1 carrier), cells must rely on error-prone backup repair pathways (like NHEJ), which introduce frequent mutations. This dramatically accelerates the accumulation of driver mutations needed for carcinogenesis (the multi-hit model). BRCA1 carriers also have elevated ovarian cancer risk. Notably, BRCA1-deficient cancers are particularly sensitive to PARP inhibitors (like olaparib) — a synthetic lethality approach where blocking the backup repair pathway kills only cells lacking BRCA1.
